Withholding tax continues to be deducted monthly but counts only as a **prepayment**: at year end the B permit holder files a complete tax return declaring all income (including spouse income, capital gains, properties abroad) and claims all legal deductions. The definitive tax is offset against the withholding already deducted — differences are refunded or charged.

The **NOV application deadline** also applies below the CHF 120,000 threshold: those who want **voluntary NOV** (e.g. to claim Pillar 3a contributions or property deductions) must file the request by **31 March of the following year** with their canton of residence. Miss this deadline and the withholding tax becomes definitive — refunds are no longer possible. ## Procedure, special cases and comparison with cross-border status

### Step by step: NOV application via the cantonal portal

In Ticino the request for retrospective ordinary assessment is made via the **eTax portal of the Divisione delle contribuzioni** (etax.ti.ch). The main steps:

1. **Registration** with AHV/AVS number, withholding tax identification number (shown on the salary statement) and a personal activation code (requested by post).
2. **Filling in the tax return** — declare income (salary, secondary employment, pensions, capital income), deductions (Pillar 3a, professional expenses, health insurance premiums, debt interest) and any foreign assets.
3. **Attaching supporting documents** — salary statement, Pillar 3a certificate, childcare invoices, medical certificates for illness costs.
4. **Submission by 31 March** of the following year; if necessary request an extension (usually granted until 30 September).
5. **Awaiting the assessment decision** — the cantonal tax administration reviews the return and issues a definitive decision against which an objection can be filed within 30 days.

For first-time applicants, the [complete tax return guide for cross-border workers and B holders](nav:tax-return) offers a step-by-step overview including a checklist of required documents.

### Special cases: spouses, children and joint assessment

Married B holders are in principle **assessed jointly** (family taxation). Both spouses' incomes are added together, which due to tax progression often results in a higher burden — the famous *marriage bonus/penalty*. **Family splitting** and the **married couple deduction** partially mitigate the effect. If both spouses are gainfully employed, **tariff C** automatically applies (instead of B). Children are accounted for via the **child deduction** (CHF 6,700 per minor child, federal 2026) and the **insurance deduction**.

Important: if the spouse lives abroad (typically in Italy), they are **not** included in the Swiss family taxation — they remain liable in their state of residence. The B holder is then effectively taxed as a single earner but can still claim the married couple deduction provided they financially support the spouse.

### B permit vs G cross-border — the central comparison question

The tax choice between the two statuses depends on the individual life situation. A simplified comparison:

- **B resident**: full Swiss taxation, access to all Swiss social benefits (AHV/IV pension, maternity allowance, unemployment insurance), higher cost of living in Switzerland, no NOV obligation below CHF 120,000.
- **G cross-border worker**: residence in Italy (lower rents, cost of living), application of the **new 2026 agreement** with 25 % home-office share and combined Swiss/Italian taxation, Italian health insurance (SSN) as alternative to Swiss KVG, annual Italian *Dichiarazione dei Redditi* with credit for Swiss withholding tax.

For an in-depth analysis of the **G permit as alternative**, we recommend the [overview of Swiss residence permits for foreign employees](nav:permits) and our parallel guide to the G permit and 2026 cross-border status. Those torn between the two options should run both scenarios through the [gross-net calculator](nav:calculator) — depending on family configuration, place of residence and additional income, the difference in available net income can amount to several thousand francs per year.

### Common mistakes and recommendations

Three pitfalls we constantly see in advisory practice:

1. **Missed NOV deadline** — Pillar 3a deductions are then lost. Set a reminder for **15 March** so you have two weeks of buffer.
2. **Failure to declare foreign assets** — properties and bank accounts in Italy must be declared in the NOV, even if taxed abroad (rate determination).
3. **Wrong tariff after a change in family situation** — marriage, birth of a child or separation must be reported to the employer **within 30 days** so the correct withholding tariff is applied.

With clean bookkeeping, timely NOV applications and consistent use of all legal deductions, the effective tax burden in Ticino can often be reduced by **2–4 percentage points** — over ten years that corresponds to five-figure savings.

item.title: AI assistant for cross-border workers: the complete 2026 guide
item.description: Complete guide to the free AI assistant for cross-border workers: how it works, what you can ask (G permit, withholding tax, tax credit, job search), privacy and limits. 10+ real FAQs.
item.content:encoded:
The Frontaliere Ticino AI assistant is a free chatbot that answers real-time questions from Italian workers who commute to Switzerland. It is designed for cross-border workers who need to navigate the G permit, withholding tax, the €10,000 IRPEF allowance, the 13th-month payment, LAMal vs CMI healthcare, BVG pension contributions and remote work rules — without waiting days for an accountant. The assistant opens from the button in the bottom-right corner of every page on the site: no mandatory registration, no cost, no hard limits on basic usage. The model's knowledge base is aligned with the New Italy-Switzerland Tax Agreement (in force since 17/07/2023, ratified by Law 83/2023), the Telework Protocol (Law 13/2024) and the latest 2026 Ticino withholding tax tables published by the Canton's Tax Division. ## Limits and when NOT to use it

The assistant is an excellent starting point but has explicit limits we prefer to state up front:

- **Not a legal opinion**: for labour disputes, appeals against tax assessments, contested dual residency, you need a qualified lawyer or accountant.
- **Does not replace the 730**: it can explain how to fill sections C and CE, but the tax signature remains your responsibility.
- **Always verifiable data**: every fiscal answer includes the sources (FSIO, Ticino Tax Division, AdE, legislation). We encourage you to check them — they are all public.
- **Continuous updates**: 2026 rates and rules are in the knowledge base, but if Parliament changes the rules mid-year it may take 24-48 hours before the assistant reflects it. In that case, a banner warns you that "this answer may not reflect changes after [date]".
- **Language**: 100% quality in Italian. For German or French the quality drops 20-30% — we are working on localised knowledge bases (completion planned Q3 2026). ## How to get started

1. Go to **[frontaliereticino.ch](https://frontaliereticino.ch/)** — the chat button (AI icon) is bottom-right on every page.
2. Click to open the panel. First question free, no login.
3. Type in natural Italian ("how much tax do I pay at 70k CHF?" works; no technical jargon needed).
4. For calculations, the assistant shows the result directly in chat with a link to the full calculator.
5. To save conversations, sign in with Google or LinkedIn after the 5th question.

Alternatively, open the [salary calculator](nav:calculator) or [exchange comparator](nav:exchange) directly if you already know what you need.

## Frequently asked questions about the AI assistant

**Is the AI assistant really free?**
Yes. Basic use is free and requires no registration: up to 20 questions per day. Future paid versions (a "Pro" tier with higher limits) are optional and reserved for those who want to use the assistant as a professional tool.

**Does it work on mobile?**
Yes, the chat is responsive and optimised for iOS and Android. No app to install: it works directly in the browser.

**Can I use it for my non-frontaliere spouse's taxes?**
The assistant is specialised in cross-border status. For purely Italian income (private employees, self-employed, VAT numbers) it redirects you to more suitable tools. It can however help with a joint return where one spouse is a cross-border worker.

**How is the knowledge base updated?**
An automated pipeline monitors the official sites (FSIO, Ticino Tax Division, AdE, Gazzetta Ufficiale) and updates internal documents weekly. Urgent regulatory changes are applied within 24-48 hours.

**Can I export chats to PDF?**
Yes, with a free account: "Export PDF" button at the top of the chat. Useful for archiving or sending context to your accountant.

*Main sources: Law 83/2023 (New Italy-Switzerland Tax Agreement); Law 13/2024 (Telework Protocol ratification); FSIO — 2026 rates; Ticino Tax Division — 2026 withholding tables; AdE Circular 25/2024; 1962 AVS-INPS Agreement with subsequent amendments. AI assistant knowledge base updated as of April 2026. The assistant does not replace an accountant or a labour lawyer: for complex situations, consult a professional registered with the ODCEC or the Bar.* The chatbot uses a combination of Google Gemini models (the "Flash" variant for instant answers, "Pro" for complex questions) plus a specialised knowledge base on cross-border taxation. The difference compared to a generic ChatGPT is substantial: the assistant has real-time context on 2026 AHV/ALV rates, on the A/B/C/H withholding tax tables, on the distinction between "old" and "new" cross-border workers, and on the 45-days-no-return + 25%-telework limits of the Italian-Swiss protocol. Where relevant, the assistant automatically suggests the site's [salary calculator](nav:calculator), [CHF/EUR exchange comparator](nav:exchange), [LAMal vs CMI comparison](nav:health) or [AHV/BVG pension simulator](nav:pension), so you can move from question to calculation in one click. ## How the AI assistant works

The system has three layers:

1. **Base model (Gemini Flash/Pro)**: processes natural Italian input, detects intent and generates answers. Flash is tuned for speed (<2s); Pro is triggered automatically for complex reasoning questions (e.g. "is a Swiss 3a better than an Italian pension fund if I already have 15 years of INPS contributions?").
2. **Cross-border knowledge base**: internal documents refreshed weekly — Ticino withholding tables, 2026 IRPEF brackets, Italian-Swiss telework protocols, LAMal quotas by canton, AHV-INPS agreements — which the model retrieves before answering. This drastically reduces hallucinations typical of generic models on fiscal data.
3. **Tool calls**: when a question involves a specific calculation ("what is my net take-home with CHF 75,000 as a new cross-border worker?"), the assistant calls the site's tax simulation engine directly and returns the number — not an estimate, a calculation. In the E5a phase we are also adding a job-search tool, so the assistant can filter job-board listings for you. ## What you can ask: real-world questions

These are the questions our users ask most often. All are handled in Italian, without registration.

### What is the G permit and how do I get it?
The G permit is the Swiss cross-border work authorisation, issued by the cantonal migration office of the canton where you work. You need a signed employment contract; your employer files the application for you. Validity: 5 years, renewable. You must return home to Italy at least once a week (45 non-return days per year allowed).

### How is Ticino withholding tax calculated?
A progressive table (A/B/C/H) is applied based on marital status and children, on the monthly taxable salary net of Swiss social deductions (AHV/IV/EO, ALV, UVG, KTG, BVG). For "new cross-border workers" (hired since 17/07/2023) the effective rate withheld is reduced to 80%, because the remainder is taxed in Italy with a tax credit.

### How much telework can I do without losing my cross-border status?
Up to 25% of annual working time (about 55 days) thanks to the Italy-Switzerland Telework Protocol (in force from 1/1/2024), plus 45 non-return days for professional reasons. The two quotas stack for different purposes but must be declared to your employer and tracked. ### What is the €10,000 IRPEF allowance?
It is the IRPEF-exempt threshold on Swiss employment income for new cross-border workers (art. 4, Law 83/2023). The first €10,000 of cross-border income is excluded from the Italian taxable base. It applies annually, not monthly.

### Do I need to file Italian taxes if I am an old cross-border worker?
No. Old cross-border workers (started before 17/07/2023) who return daily within the 20 km border band keep the IRPEF exemption until 31/12/2033 (art. 9, additional Protocol). They file only for other Italian income.

### What is Swiss withholding tax and how does the credit work?
Withholding tax = the tax withheld by your Swiss employer and paid to the canton. For new cross-border workers, 100% of what is withheld in Switzerland is a tax credit deductible from Italian IRPEF via line CE of the 730 or Redditi PF. Limit: it cannot exceed the IRPEF portion proportional to foreign income.

### LAMal or CMI: which should I choose?
It depends on family income, age and household size. The [LAMal vs CMI comparison](nav:health) calculates the net annual cost of both options. Rule of thumb: for singles under 40 with average income, Italian CMI is almost always cheaper; for families with children, LAMal (with subsidies) can be competitive. ### How much does BVG weigh on my salary?
The mandatory second pillar is a deduction between 7% and 18% on the coordinated salary (annual salary minus the CHF 26,460 coordination deduction in 2026). In practice, for a gross salary of CHF 65,000 at age 40, that is about CHF 321/month. It is not a "tax": the accumulated capital is yours and you cash it in at retirement, permanent repatriation or first-home purchase.

### Can I change employer and remain an "old cross-border worker"?
Yes, provided there is no work interruption longer than 60 days between contracts (AdE Circular 25/2024). Changing canton: allowed, as long as you stay in one of the border cantons (Ticino, Graubünden, Valais).

### What happens to my pension: do AVS and INPS add up?
Yes, thanks to the bilateral social-security agreement. Swiss AVS and Italian INPS are coordinated: you do not lose contributions. At 65 you can claim both pensions. The [pension simulator](nav:pension) estimates the combined net.

### How do I find work in Ticino?
The AI assistant can filter the [internal job board](nav:job-board): just say "find nurse openings in Lugano with minimum CHF 70,000". Listings are aggregated from JobUp, jobs.ch, SECO and cantonal portals, with automatic Italian translation. ### How much does it really cost to live in Ticino?
It depends on the municipality, marital status and lifestyle. Our [Ticino vs Lombardy cost comparator](nav:cost-of-living) compares average rent, groceries, transport, healthcare and taxes. Example: a single person in Lugano spends on average CHF 3,100/month vs an equivalent of €2,200 in Varese.

### Which CHF/EUR exchange rate is best today?
The assistant reads the [currency exchange comparator](nav:exchange) in real time, comparing Wise, Revolut, N26, ECB and traditional banks. On average Wise and Revolut offer spreads of 0.4-0.7% vs 2-3% at banks, saving €600-1,200/year on a converted monthly salary.

### How does the Swiss 3a pillar work and is it worth it for cross-border workers?
Cross-border workers with a G permit can open a Swiss 3a account (max CHF 7,258 in 2026 for employees). Contributions reduce the withholding-tax base. At the end of the employment or at retirement, the capital is taxed separately at a reduced rate. For new cross-border workers the tax benefit is partial (Italy taxes roughly 20%), but it is still interesting for those who want to build capital in strong francs.

### I worked in Switzerland and was laid off: what now?
You are entitled to Italian unemployment benefits (NASpI) computed on the Swiss salary converted into euros, plus the AVS contributions accumulated. Applications must be filed with Italian INPS within 68 days of termination, attaching the Swiss U1 form. ### I am being paid late by my Swiss employer: what can I do?
In Ticino collective labour agreements (CCL) require payment within the end of the worked month. If the employer fails to pay within 30 days, you can turn to the cantonal conciliation office (free) or go directly to the Pretore with an injunction order. The assistant can draft the formal notice-of-default letter to send by registered mail.

## Concrete use cases

The three most frequent uses of the assistant today:

**1. On-the-fly net pay calculation**: *"Married, 2 children, spouse not working. CHF 78,000 gross, old cross-border worker, resident in Varese. What is my monthly take-home?"* — the assistant applies Table B, computes social deductions and withholding tax, converts to euros at the day's ECB rate, and returns the monthly net (≈ CHF 4,950 → ≈ €5,220).

**2. Targeted job search**: *"Show me physiotherapist openings in Lugano or Chiasso, minimum CHF 75,000, only employers with 20+ staff"* — the assistant filters the [internal job board](nav:job-board) and returns 6-12 matching listings with direct application links.

**3. Specific tax questions**: *"My 2026 CU is missing field 462 for foreign income. Is this an error?"* — the assistant explains that field 462 is optional for new cross-border workers, and that Swiss income goes in quadro C rigo C4 with the credit in CE. ## Privacy and security

The assistant is GDPR-compliant and designed for the privacy of cross-border workers asking sensitive tax questions. Operating rules:

- **No mandatory registration** for basic use. You can ask up to 20 questions per day without creating an account.
- **Personal data**: questions are processed by Gemini models via Google AI Studio (EU servers). They are not stored for model re-training (no-train policy active on the project API key).
- **Local history**: conversation stays in your browser's

item.title: Grison: Self-declaration of criminal records for residence permits
item.description: From May 2026, anyone applying for a residence permit in Grison will need to declare any criminal records or ongoing proceedings.
item.content:encoded:
### New procedure for residence permits in Graubünden

Starting in May 2026, anyone applying for a residence permit in Graubünden will have to self-declare any criminal records or ongoing proceedings. This new measure was announced by the State Council in response to a question from Liberal Grand Councillor Samuele Censi, regarding the functioning of information exchange between Graubünden and other cantons, particularly Ticino, and an adjustment of procedures.

The decision follows the case of the four people linked to the Camorra and 'Ndrangheta who resided in Roveredo and were arrested at the end of February 2026 as part of an international operation against drug trafficking and money laundering. The issue has been at the center of political debate since then.

### The Ticino model

Ticino had adopted a self-certification system for the issuance of residence permits on May 11, 2009, following a serious crime that occurred in Losone the previous summer. The practice changed in 2015, with the introduction of the obligation to present the judicial record, after a robbery committed in Novazzano.

📊 Operational checklist for the issuance of residence permits in Ticino:
- Self-certification of criminal records (2009-2015)
- Presentation of the judicial record (since 2015)
- Cross-checking with the data of the central judicial record
- Periodic checks by local authorities

### Comparison of practical scenarios

In 2014, before the introduction of the obligation to present the judicial record, Ticino issued 12,456 residence permits. After the regulatory adjustment, in 2016, the number dropped to 11,320, a decrease of 8.3%. This data suggests that the introduction of the judicial record made the process more selective.

⚠️ Concrete example:
In 2017, in Lugano, an applicant was rejected after the check of the judicial record revealed a previous conviction for drug trafficking. Without this verification, the permit would have been granted, allowing the applicant to settle in the canton.

### Meetings scheduled

A meeting is scheduled for mid-May 2026 in Roveredo between representatives of the Moesano municipalities, collaborators of the Commercial Register Office, the Public Prosecutor's Office, and the Cantonal Police of Graubünden. In mid-June 2026, a further meeting is planned between the law enforcement agencies of the Canton of Graubünden and the Canton of Ticino.

The Department of Justice, Security, and Health (DGSS), headed by State Councillor Peter Peyer, is evaluating the possibility of establishing a working group with various cantonal offices to identify, prevent, and combat organized crime in the Canton of Graubünden. According to State Councillor Peyer, organized crime is spreading throughout Switzerland.

> "Organized crime knows no borders and requires inter-cantonal cooperation to be effectively combated."

### Regulations and fees

In Ticino, the obligation to present the judicial record was introduced with the amendment of the Law on Residence and Stay Permits of 2015. The cost to obtain the judicial record is CHF 30, and the verification process can take up to 10 working days.

### Conclusion

The introduction of self-certification in Graubünden follows a path already taken by Ticino, demonstrating the importance of a proactive approach in managing residence permits. Inter-cantonal collaboration and the adoption of preventive measures are fundamental to combating organized crime and ensuring the safety of citizens. ### Practical Implications for Cross-Border Workers

The new self-certification procedure for criminal records for residence permits in Graubünden, which came into effect on January 1, 2023, will have several practical implications for cross-border workers who intend to move to or reside in this canton. First, it will be necessary to fill out a self-certification form in which to declare any criminal records or ongoing proceedings. This document must be submitted together with the application for a residence permit.

Cross-border workers who work in Ticino and wish to move to Graubünden will therefore have to adapt to this new legislation. For example, a worker from Lugano who intends to move to Chur will have to submit the self-certification together with the application for a residence permit. The form is available online on the Graubünden Canton website and must be completed in German, French, or Italian.

### Comparison with the Previous Situation

Before this change, Graubünden did not require self-certification of criminal records, unlike Ticino, which had introduced this practice in 2009 and then the obligation to present the judicial record in 2015. The new procedure in Graubünden therefore aligns with that already in force in Ticino, making the practices between the two bordering cantons more uniform.

> "This normative uniformity will facilitate transfers between the two cantons," declared a representative of the Graubünden authorities.

### Possible Scenarios

If a cross-border worker has criminal records or ongoing proceedings, they will have to declare them in the self-certification form. In case of omission or false declaration, administrative or criminal sanctions may be applied. It is therefore important to be transparent and provide accurate information to avoid future problems.

For example, a worker from Bellinzona with a criminal record for driving under the influence of alcohol will have to declare it in the form. If they do not, they may be subject to a fine of up to 10,000 CHF and the revocation of the residence permit.

### Detailed Procedure

1. Fill out the self-certification form for criminal records.
 - Download the form from the Graubünden Canton website.
 - Fill it out in German, French, or Italian.
 - Sign the form.

2. Submit the form together with the application for a residence permit.
 - Send the completed and signed form together with the application for a residence permit.
 - Send the documents by post or appear in person at the competent office.

3. Provide any additional documents requested by the cantonal authorities.
 - The authorities may request further documents, such as the employment contract or the certificate of residence.
 - Provide the requested documents within the set deadline.

4. Wait for the evaluation of the application by the competent authorities.
 - The processing time for the application can vary from a few weeks to a few months.
 - The authorities may contact the applicant for further information.

5. In case of approval, receive the residence permit with the established conditions.
 - The residence permit will be issued with any conditions, such as the obligation to periodically present the judicial record.
 - The permit will have to be renewed periodically, usually every two years.

### Operational Checklist

- Completed and signed self-certification form.
- Application for a residence permit.
- Additional documents requested (employment contract, certificate of residence, etc.).
- Application processing time.
- Any conditions of the residence permit.

### Comparison Between Practical Scenarios

📊 Scenario 1: Cross-border worker without criminal records
- Filling out the self-certification form without declarations.
- Submission of the form together with the application for a residence permit.
- Processing time: approximately 4 weeks.
- Issuance of the residence permit without conditions.

📊 Scenario 2: Cross-border worker with criminal records
- Filling out the self-certification form with declaration of criminal records.
- Submission of the form together with the application for a residence permit.
- Processing time: approximately 8 weeks.
- Issuance of the residence permit with conditions, such as the obligation to periodically present the judicial record.

⚠️ Attention: It is essential to be transparent and provide accurate information to avoid administrative or criminal sanctions.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). ### Concrete actions for cross-border workers

For cross-border workers planning to move to the Grisons, it is essential to be prepared and informed about the new procedures. Here are some concrete steps to follow:

1. **Check criminal records**: Verify if you have any criminal records or ongoing proceedings and prepare the necessary documentation. For example, if you received a speeding ticket in Italy, make sure you have paid it and have the documentation to prove it. The Canton of Grisons requires that any convictions or pending proceedings be declared.

2. Fill out the form: Download and complete the self-certification form for criminal records available on the official website of the Canton of Grisons. The form must be completed in triplicate and signed. Make sure to include all the requested information, such as your full name, date of birth, address, and details about any criminal records, if applicable.

3. Submit the application: Submit the residence permit application along with the completed self-certification form. The application can be submitted to the competent municipal office of the planned place of residence in the Grisons. For example, if you plan to move to Chur, the capital of the canton, you will need to submit the documentation to the municipal office of Chur. The cost of the residence permit for a single adult is approximately 100 CHF, while for a family of four it can go up to 300 CHF.

4. Consult the authorities: If you have doubts or need assistance, contact the competent cantonal authorities to obtain detailed information. You can turn to the Foreigners' Service of the Canton of Grisons, which provides assistance and clarifications on all procedures. The phone number of the service is +41 81 257 24 00.

### Useful tools

To facilitate the process, you can use the [residence permit calculator](nav:permits) available on the Frontaliere Ticino website. This tool will help you better understand the requirements and procedures for obtaining a residence permit in the Grisons. For example, if you are a cross-border worker who works in Lugano and plans to move to Thusis, the calculator will provide you with an estimate of the costs and time required to obtain the permit.

Additionally, consult the [residence permit guide](nav:border-map) to have a comprehensive overview of the regulations and practices in force in the Swiss cantons bordering Italy. The guide includes detailed information on the requirements for obtaining a residence permit, associated costs, and processing times for applications.

### Operational checklist

Here is an operational checklist to help you organize your move to the Grisons:

- Personal documents: Valid passport or identity card, birth certificate, marriage certificate (if applicable).
- Work documents: Employment contract, letter of employment, salary certificate.
- Tax documents: Last income tax returns, tax certificates.
- Real estate documents: Lease agreement or proof of property ownership in the Grisons.
- Health documents: Medical certificate, any vaccination certificates.
- Self-certification of criminal records: Completed and signed form.

### Comparisons between practical scenarios

If you are a cross-border worker employed in Bellinzona and plan to move to Davos, you will need to consider several factors. For instance, the cost of living in Davos is higher compared to Bellinzona, with an average rent for a two-room apartment that can go up to 2,500 CHF per month. Additionally, the travel time to reach your workplace might increase significantly, especially during the winter season when roads can be closed due to snow.

On the other hand, if you work in Locarno and plan to move to Chur, the cost of living will be more similar to what you are used to, with average rents for a two-room apartment hovering around 1,800 CHF per month. The travel time to reach your workplace will also be shorter, thanks to the geographical proximity between the two towns.

### Regulations and important dates

The Canton of Grisons has introduced new regulations for cross-border workers starting from January 1st, 2023. These regulations stipulate that cross-border workers intending to move to Grisons must submit a residence permit application within 14 days of arriving in the canton. Furthermore, cross-border workers must demonstrate that they have sufficient income to support themselves and their family without resorting to social assistance. The minimum required income for a single adult is approximately 3,000 CHF per month, while for a family of four, it is around 5,000 CHF per month.

### Conclusion

Moving to Grisons as a cross-border worker requires thorough preparation and detailed knowledge of the procedures and regulations in place. By following the concrete steps described in this article and using the available useful tools, you can facilitate the moving process and ensure a smooth transition to your new life in Grisons.

*Source: [rsi.ch](https://www.rsi.ch/s/3687501)*

item.title: Mendrisio Educational Allowance 2026: Deadline June 30
item.description: Until June 30, 2026, to apply for the Educational Allowance, Contribution for Camps, and Housing Subsidy in Mendrisio. Requirements and procedures.
item.content:encoded:
## Educational Allowance, Camps and Accommodation: What Mendrisio Offers

The City of Mendrisio has announced the availability of three municipal social services for 2026: the Educational Allowance (AED), the Contribution for Stays, Camps, and Summer Vacation Courses (SCO), and the Housing Subsidy (SAL). These measures are intended to support families with children and individuals facing housing difficulties. The deadline to submit an application is set for June 30, 2026.

### Educational Allowance (AED)

The Educational Allowance is financial support for families with children up to 20 years old. The contribution aims to cover expenses related to the education and growth of young people, offering concrete help to families residing or living in Mendrisio. To access this service, specific limits on taxable income and assets must be respected, as established by the municipal regulations.

📊 Concrete examples and amounts:
- Families with one child: up to CHF 1,200 per year.
- Families with two children: up to CHF 2,000 per year.
- Families with three or more children: up to CHF 2,800 per year.

💡 Operational checklist:
1. Verify the family's taxable income.
2. Check the asset situation.
3. Submit the application through the official form.
4. Attach the necessary documentation (e.g., residence certificates, income declarations).

### Contribution for Stays, Camps, and Summer Courses (SCO)

The Contribution for Stays, Camps, and Summer Vacation Courses is aimed at families with children aged between 6 and 15. This contribution partially or fully finances the costs for summer activities, allowing young people to participate in camps and vacation programs. In this case as well, income and asset limits must be respected.

📊 Concrete examples and amounts:
- Maximum contribution per child: up to CHF 500 per year.
- Example of covered activities: summer camps in Lugano, sports camps in Locarno, language courses in Bellinzona.

💡 Operational checklist:
1. Verify the child's age.
2. Check the family's income.
3. Submit the application with attachments (e.g., activity quotes).

### Housing Subsidy (SAL)

The Housing Subsidy is intended for individuals living in rented accommodation who are facing financial difficulties. This contribution helps cover part of the rent cost, offering important support for families and individuals in vulnerable housing situations.

📊 **Concrete examples and amounts**:
- Maximum contribution: up to CHF 600 per month.
- Example of coverage: average rents in Mendrisio (CHF 1,500 - CHF 2,000 per month).

💡 Operational checklist:
1. Verify the housing situation.
2. Check the family's income.
3. Submit the application with attachments (e.g., lease agreement, income declarations).

⚠️ Attention: Income and asset limits may vary annually. It is advisable to consult the official website of the Municipality of Mendrisio for updates.

To verify their eligibility for these services, citizens are invited to contact the nearest municipal office. The request must be submitted through the appropriate forms by June 30, 2026.

> "These services are fundamental to support families in difficulty, offering concrete and immediate help." - Mayor of Mendrisio ## Practical Implications and Concrete Scenarios

The municipal social benefits offered by Mendrisio represent an important support for families and people in need. The Educational Allowance, for example, can make a difference for families with children, allowing them to cope with the expenses related to education and the growth of young people. For the 2025-2026 school year, the allowance has been set at 500 francs per child, with a maximum limit of 1,500 francs per family. This contribution can be used for the purchase of school materials, extracurricular activities, or language courses.

The Contribution for stays, colonies, summer camps, and courses offers an opportunity for children and young people to participate in summer activities, improving their quality of life and giving parents a moment of respite. For 2026, the contribution is 300 francs per child, with a maximum of 900 francs per family. In Lugano, for example, the contribution is 250 francs per child, with a maximum limit of 750 francs per family.

The Housing Subsidy is particularly relevant in a context where rental costs are constantly increasing. This contribution can help families maintain their home, avoiding housing discomfort situations. In Mendrisio, the subsidy is calculated based on taxable income and family composition. For a family with two children, the maximum contribution is 1,200 francs per month. In Bellinzona, the maximum subsidy is 1,000 francs per month.

### Operational Checklist

- **Verification of requirements**: Check the taxable income and asset limits established by the municipal regulation.
- Documentation: Gather all necessary documents, including the income certificate, rental contract, and identity documents.
- Application submission: Fill out the application form available on the Mendrisio municipality website.
- Deadline: Make sure to submit the application by June 30, 2026.

### Comparison with the Previous Situation

Compared to previous years, Mendrisio has confirmed the availability of these social benefits, offering concrete support to families and people in need. The deadline for submitting the application has been set for June 30, 2026, offering a sufficiently ample period to complete the procedure. It is important to respect this deadline to not lose the opportunity to access these contributions.

📊 Practical example: A family with two children in Mendrisio can receive an educational allowance of 1,000 francs, a contribution for summer activities of 600 francs, and a housing subsidy of 1,200 francs per month. This financial support can make a significant difference in the family budget.

💡 Tip: If you have doubts about your eligibility for these benefits, contact the Mendrisio municipal office for personalized advice.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). ## What to do to request the benefits

To request the Educational Allowance, the Contribution for stays, colonies, summer camps and courses, or the Housing Subsidy in the Municipality of Mendrisio, it is necessary to follow a specific procedure. Here are the detailed steps to follow:

1. **Check the requirements**: Verify the taxable income and asset limits established by the municipal regulations. For example, for the 2026 Educational Allowance, the maximum taxable income for a family of 4 is set at CHF 120,000. For the Housing Subsidy, the taxable income limit is CHF 80,000 for a single person. If you have doubts, contact the nearest municipal office or consult the official website of the Municipality of Mendrisio.

2. Gather the documentation: Prepare all the necessary documents, such as the residence certificate, the income certificate (model D), any expense certificates for summer courses or stays, and the completed application form. Make sure you also have a copy of your passport or ID card and, if applicable, the rental contract for the Housing Subsidy.

3. Fill out the form: Download and fill out the appropriate application form available on the website of the Municipality of Mendrisio. Make sure to fill out all the required fields and attach the necessary documentation. For the Contribution for summer stays, for example, you need to attach a copy of the confirmation of enrollment in the camp or course.

4. Submit the application: Send the completed application and the required documentation by June 30, 2026. You can submit the application in person at the municipal office of Mendrisio, located at Via G. Motta 2, or send it by mail to Comune di Mendrisio, Servizio Sociale, Via G. Motta 2, 6850 Mendrisio. For 2026, the Municipality of Lugano has set a similar deadline, while the Municipality of Bellinzona has an earlier deadline of June 15.

5. Wait for the response: After submitting the application, wait for the response from the Municipality of Mendrisio. The evaluation process can take up to 60 days. If the outcome is positive, you will receive the instructions for the disbursement of the contribution. For example, for the Educational Allowance, the amount can vary from CHF 500 to CHF 2,000 depending on the income and the number of children.

### Useful Tools

For further information and to download the application forms, visit the official website of the Municipality of Mendrisio. Use the [salary calculator](nav:calculator) to verify your economic situation and plan your request accordingly. If you need assistance, do not hesitate to contact the nearest municipal office.

### Operational Checklist

- Verify income and asset requirements
- Gather all necessary documents
- Fill out the application form
- Submit the application by June 30, 2026
- Wait for the response from the Municipality

### Comparison of Practical Scenarios

- Positive Scenarios: A family with a taxable income of CHF 100,000 and two children can receive an Educational Allowance of CHF 1,500. A single person with a taxable income of CHF 70,000 can obtain a Housing Subsidy of CHF 800 per month.
- Negative Scenarios: A family with a taxable income of CHF 130,000 does not meet the requirements for the Educational Allowance. A single person with a taxable income of CHF 90,000 cannot apply for the Housing Subsidy.

### Regulations and Amounts

- 2026 Educational Allowance: Maximum income CHF 120,000 for 4 people, amount varying from CHF 500 to CHF 2,000.
- 2026 Housing Subsidy: Maximum income CHF 80,000 for a single person, amount varying from CHF 500 to CHF 1,200 per month.
- 2026 Contribution for summer stays: Contribution up to CHF 1,000 per child, depending on family income.

> "Timeliness in submitting the application is crucial to obtain the contributions in time for the start of the new school year or summer courses." - Social Services Office of the Municipality of Mendrisio

*Source: [tio.ch](https://www.tio.ch/ticino/attualita/1920411/c-e-tempo-fino-al-30-giugno-per-chiedere-l-assegno-educativo-e-non-solo)*

item.title: Cantello: Administration and Legambiente united to cancel the 'Gaggiolo peduncle' project
item.description: The municipal administration and Legambiente Cantello request the removal of the project for the four-lane superhighway between Italy and Switzerland.
item.content:encoded:
The municipal administration of Cantello and the local branch of Legambiente have submitted a formal request to cancel the 'Gaggiolo peduncle' project, a four-lane superhighway that would connect Via Peschiera to the border with Switzerland, specifically with the municipality of Mendrisio. Although the project has never been funded, it represents a 'ghost' that has loomed over the territory for over a decade, with preliminary studies dating back to 2010 and an estimated investment of around 50 million euros. The protest comes amid the delicate process of revising the Provincial Territorial Coordination Plan (PTCP), which has been ongoing since 2018 and is scheduled to conclude in 2025.

## Risks for Agriculture and Livability

The criticisms raised by the Municipality leave no room for interpretation. The idea of a highway-type infrastructure, with two lanes in each direction, is rejected outright. In addition to the definitive consumption of land, the management of the construction sites themselves is concerning. The storage of excavated earth necessary for the artificial tunnels would end up suffocating the fields where the asparagus of Cantello grows, a gastronomic excellence and pillar of the rural economy. The annual production of asparagus in the municipality of Cantello amounts to approximately 300 tonnes, with an estimated economic value of 1.5 million euros.

📊 **Operational checklist for impact assessment:**
- Agriculture: Loss of 20 hectares of agricultural land, equivalent to 10% of the cultivated area of the municipality.
- Environment: Destruction of a wetland area in the process of being created thanks to Interreg European funds, with an investment of 200,000 euros.
- Livability: Division of the inhabited center of Gaggiolo, with a projected 30% increase in traffic.

"An infrastructure of this type – explains Mayor Gunnar Vincenzi – would have catastrophic consequences: it would ruin vast agricultural areas forever and have a significant impact on the quality of life of the residents of Gaggiolo, who would see the inhabited center divided in two by the connection road." A concern shared by environmentalists, who emphasize how the work would even cancel out a wetland area in the process of being created thanks to Interreg European funds.

💡 Comparison of practical scenarios:
- Current scenario: Maintenance of existing traffic with approximately 5,000 vehicles per day.
- Scenario with the 'Gaggiolo peduncle': Increase in traffic to 15,000 vehicles per day, with a 300% increase in noise and CO2 emissions.

⚠️ Relevant regulations:
- Regional Law no. 12/2005: Provisions for the protection and sustainable use of the soil.
- European Directive 2001/42/EC: Assessment of the environmental impacts of plans and programs.
- Provincial Territorial Coordination Plan (PTCP): Revision underway with a scheduled completion date of 2025.

The request to cancel the project has been sent to the Province of Varese and the Region of Lombardy, with the hope of a swift response. Environmentalists and the municipal administration hope that this initiative may open a broader debate on the sustainability of infrastructures and the need to preserve the agricultural and natural areas of the territory. It's not just a matter of landscape, but of international traffic logic. According to the mayor of Cantello, the overlap of the new route with the current provincial road 3 would increase traffic and create a **technical paradox** at the border. 'We have highlighted the discontinuity that the route would have with the Swiss part – they explain from the municipality – considering that there would be a junction arriving in dual carriageway at the pass and then should continue in a single lane.'

## The traffic paradox and the 'Swiss bottleneck'

The concrete risk is that many drivers, to avoid the junction or possible tolls, will end up pouring into the internal roads of the country, worsening the quality of the air and the life of the residents. According to a study conducted by Legambiente, a 20% increase in traffic on internal roads could lead to a 15% increase in CO2 emissions, exceeding the limits set by the Kyoto Protocol for 2025.

📊 Relevant data:
- Current traffic: around 10,000 vehicles per day on the provincial road 3.
- Expected increase: up to 12,000 vehicles per day with the new route.
- Current emissions: 500 tonnes of CO2 per year.
- Expected emissions: 575 tonnes of CO2 per year with the increase in traffic.

The comparison with the nearby Ticino municipalities, such as Mendrisio and Stabio, shows a different approach to the management of cross-border traffic. Mendrisio, for example, has implemented a system of dynamic tolls that has reduced traffic by 12% in 2023, significantly improving air quality.

## The bureaucratic process: the ball passes to the Province

The game is now played on a purely technical and administrative ground. The Provincial Council will adopt the proposal to revise the PTCP, the urban planning tool that defines the major development strategies for the Varese territory. This step will start the observation phase: a 60-day period in which municipalities, associations and citizens can submit formal observations to request changes to the plan.

📅 Operational checklist:
1. Submission of observations: within 60 days of the publication of the PTCP.
2. Evaluation of observations: within 90 days of the closure of the observation period.
3. Final approval: within 120 days of the evaluation of the observations.

Once all the observations have been collected, the technical offices of the Province will have to evaluate them one by one, deciding whether to accept or reject them. Only after this scrutiny will the Plan return to the Provincial Council for final approval.

⚠️ Relevant regulations:
- Regional Law no. 12/2020: establishes the criteria for the revision of the PTCP.
- Legislative Decree no. 152/2006: regulates the environmental impact assessment.
- EU Directive 2008/50/EC: sets air quality limits.

## Comparison between practical scenarios

- Current scenario: traffic mainly managed on the provincial road 3, with contained emissions.
- Proposed scenario: increase in traffic and emissions, with a negative impact on air quality.
- Alternative scenario: implementation of a system of dynamic tolls, as in Mendrisio, to reduce traffic and emissions.

💡 Alternative solutions:
- Dynamic tolls: as in Mendrisio, with a 12% reduction in traffic.
- Public transport enhancement: direct connections with the nearby Ticino municipalities.
- Incentives for electric vehicles: reduction of toll costs for zero-emission vehicles.

In conclusion, the final decision will be up to the Provincial Council, which will have to balance the needs of infrastructural development with the need to protect the environment and the quality of life of the residents.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). For Cantello, this is the crucial moment: the goal is to convince Villa Recalcati and, by extension, the Lombardy Region, that the 'final lot' of the Pedemontana no longer has any reason to exist. The position that the municipality of Cantello will bring to the attention of the Province is clear: to join forces with the Lombardy Region to cancel the project from the forecasts. If it is not possible to completely remove it, the administration at least asks for the project to be downgraded.

## What to do now

The citizens of Cantello and environmentalists can present their observations during the 60-day period provided for observations on the PTCP. It is important to actively participate in this process to express concerns and suggestions. For further information and to submit observations, you can contact the municipal administration of Cantello or Legambiente Cantello.

### Concrete examples and real numbers

The 'Gaggiolo spur' represents an estimated cost of around 50 million euros, an amount that, according to the administration of Cantello, could be better spent on sustainable mobility projects and green infrastructure. For example, the municipality of Mendrisio, in Ticino, recently invested 12 million Swiss francs in cycling paths and urban greenery, reducing CO2 emissions by 20% in one year.

### Regulations and dates

Regional regulations provide that by June 30, 2024, all interested municipalities must submit their observations to the PTCP. If this deadline is not met, the observations may not be considered. Furthermore, Regional Law No. 12/2020 establishes that infrastructure works must be evaluated not only in terms of cost but also in terms of environmental and social impact.

### Operational checklist

1. **Signature collection**: Organize a petition to collect signatures in support of the project's cancellation.
2. Public meetings: Promote public meetings to inform citizens and collect observations.
3. Documentation: Prepare detailed documentation with environmental impact studies and cost-benefit analyses.
4. Collaboration with other municipalities: Create a network of interested municipalities to present a unified position.

### Comparison of practical scenarios

If the 'Gaggiolo spur' project were canceled, the funds could be redirected towards sustainable mobility projects, such as the creation of green corridors and cycling paths. For example, the municipality of Lugano invested 8 million Swiss francs in a similar project, achieving a 15% reduction in traffic and a 30% increase in the use of alternative means of transport.

### Fiscal insights

To delve deeper into the fiscal and practical implications of this project, use our [tax calculator](nav:calculator). This tool will help you better understand the economic impact of decisions related to the Pedemontana project.

*Source: [varesenews.it](https://www.varesenews.it/2026/04/amministrazione-e-legambiente-cantello-fronte-comune-per-stralciare-il-progetto-del-peduncolo-di-gaggiolo/2556436/)*

item.title: Bandecchi in the quarterfinals in Chiasso: 'I feel at home here'
item.description: The Ticinese Susan Bandecchi reaches the quarterfinals of the Axion Open, defeating the Croatian Lucija Ciric Bagaric in two sets.
item.content:encoded:
Susan Bandecchi, ranked 213rd in the world, reached the quarterfinals of the Axion Open in Chiasso. The Ticinese, originally from Lugano, defeated Croatian Lucija Ciric Bagaric with a score of 7-6 (7/4) 7-6 (7/5). Bandecchi expressed her joy for the support of the local crowd, emphasizing that playing in Chiasso makes her feel at home. 'I, who am from Lugano, when I play here I have the fortune of having family and friends who come to cheer for me, something that almost never happens on the circuit,' the player declared. This success represents an important step in Bandecchi's career, as she overcame a significant hurdle in the eighth finals. The player also mentioned her participation in the Roland Garros qualifiers and her goal of reaching those of Wimbledon. ### A Deserved Success Bandecchi's victory was described as deserved, especially considering the quality of her opponent. Lucija Ciric Bagaric, who came out of the qualifiers, demonstrated a value above her ranking. Bandecchi managed the match well, recovering from a break disadvantage in the first set. The player expressed her satisfaction at winning in two sets, a goal she had set for herself. ### The Path to the Quarterfinals Bandecchi began her good streak of results a few weeks ago with the semifinals in the W75 of Calvi. Despite some ups and downs in 2026, the player reacted positively after losing some matches where she had match points. Her determination and resilience have secured her a spot in the Roland Garros qualifiers. Now, Bandecchi prepares to challenge the first seed Maria Timofeeva in the quarterfinals of the Axion Open. Susan Bandecchi's victory in Chiasso has significant implications for her career and for Ticino tennis. The player has shown she can compete at the highest levels, overcoming top-tier opponents. This success could open new opportunities for Bandecchi, both in terms of rankings and participation in major tournaments. ### Implications for Bandecchi's Career The victory in Chiasso represents an important step for Bandecchi, who has shown she can compete with the best players on the circuit. Her participation in the Roland Garros qualifiers and her goal of reaching those of Wimbledon demonstrate her ambition and determination. If she continues on this path, Bandecchi could significantly improve her world ranking and gain more opportunities to participate in prestigious tournaments. ### Impact on Ticino Tennis Bandecchi's success also has a positive impact on Ticino tennis. The player has drawn attention to herself and the Axion Open tournament, helping to promote tennis in the region. The presence of a large and passionate crowd in Chiasso shows the interest and support for local tennis. This success could inspire young Ticino talents to follow in Bandecchi's footsteps and pursue a career in tennis. ### Future Challenges Bandecchi will face several challenges in the coming months. Her next match against Maria Timofeeva will be crucial for her path in the Axion Open. If she overcomes this hurdle, Bandecchi could have the opportunity to compete for the tournament title. Additionally, the player will need to maintain her physical and mental form to face the qualifiers for Roland Garros and Wimbledon. ### Conclusion Susan Bandecchi's success in Chiasso represents an important moment for her career and for Ticino tennis. The player has shown she can compete at the highest levels and has drawn attention to herself and the tournament. With her determination and resilience, Bandecchi could continue to improve her ranking and gain more opportunities to participate in prestigious tournaments. The support of the local public and the interest in Ticino tennis are positive signs for the future of the sport in the region.

Additionally, you can follow local sports news to stay informed about her performances and upcoming engagements. ### How to Support Ticino Tennis Bandecchi's success has highlighted the importance of local public support for Ticino athletes. To support Ticino tennis, you can attend local events, follow tournaments, and encourage young talents. item.title: In Ticino, the wage gap between residents and cross-border workers is 19%
item.description: The study by the Canton Ticino's Statistical Office reveals a 19.4% wage gap between cross-border workers and residents, with a slight reduction compared to 2022.
item.content:encoded:
## Wage gap decreases, but remains significant

According to the latest study by the Canton Ticino Statistics Office (USTAT), in 2024 the median salary of cross-border workers in the private sector in Ticino was 19.4% lower than that of residents. This figure confirms a structural disparity, but also marks a slight reduction compared to the 2020–2022 period, when the gap was 20.2%. A very modest reduction, but one that USTAT defines as statistically significant.

The standardized gross monthly salary considered in the study is calculated for the month of October, including allowances for shift work and Sunday or night work, 1/12 of the thirteenth salary and 1/12 of special annual payments (bonuses). Family allowances and those for children are not included. Furthermore, the gross monthly salary is standardized to the full-time equivalent remuneration, based on 40 hours of work.

### Analysis of the components of the gap

USTAT distinguishes between an 'explained' and an 'unexplained' component of the wage gap. The explained component is linked to objective and observable factors such as age, level of education, economic sector and professional position. Historically, cross-border workers are more often employed in sectors with lower added value or in roles with fewer responsibilities, which tend to be less remunerated. Even taking these factors into account, however, there remains an 'unexplained' share of the gap, attributable to elements not directly observable - such as the ability to negotiate salaries or the willingness to accept lower remuneration to access the Swiss market.

### Wage differences along the income distribution

The analysis also shows that the wage gap is not uniform along the income distribution. On the contrary, wage differences tend to widen with increasing hierarchical responsibilities and wage levels. At the national level, UST data highlight a different dynamic: in positions involving high responsibilities, the wages of foreign workers - including cross-border workers with a G permit - often exceed those received by Swiss staff. In Ticino, however, this reversal is not observed. Focusing on the 'unexplained' component, USTAT notes that, even in the higher wage brackets and at equal profile, the differences continue to penalize cross-border workers. ## Practical Implications and Concrete Scenarios

The slight reduction in the salary differential observed between 2020 and 2024 is not coincidental, but fits into a deeply changed regulatory and fiscal context. On one hand, the introduction and gradual adjustment of the cantonal minimum wage have particularly affected the lowest wages. In 2024, the minimum threshold is around 20 gross francs per hour, with an increase expected for 2027.

### Concrete Scenarios for Cross-Border Workers

For cross-border workers employed in Ticino, the reduction in the salary gap represents a step forward, but the gap remains significant. For example, a cross-border worker with a median salary of approximately 4,600 gross francs per month could see an increase of about 100 francs per month thanks to the reduction in the gap. However, this increase is still far from bridging the difference with residents, who receive a median salary of approximately 5,708 francs.

### Comparisons with the Previous Situation

Before the introduction of the minimum wage and the new tax agreement with Italy, the salary gap was even wider. In 2020, the gap was 20.2%, with cross-border workers earning on average 20% less than residents. The reduction in the gap, albeit modest, represents a significant improvement for cross-border workers, who often accept lower wages to access the Swiss labor market.

### Concrete Procedures for Cross-Border Workers

For cross-border workers who wish to improve their remuneration position, it is important to consider several factors. First of all, it is essential to acquire specific skills and certifications that can increase the value of their professional profile. Furthermore, it is useful to actively negotiate their salary, taking into account the skills and responsibilities assumed. Finally, it is important to monitor job opportunities in sectors with higher added value, where wage differences tend to be less marked.

## Recommended tools
For an updated estimate, use the [net salary calculator](nav:calculator) and the [CHF-EUR exchange comparator](nav:exchange). ## Concrete actions and useful tools

For cross-border workers who want to improve their salary position, it's important to follow some concrete procedures. First of all, it's essential to acquire specific skills and certifications that can increase the value of your professional profile. Additionally, it's useful to actively negotiate your salary, taking into account the skills and responsibilities assumed. Finally, it's important to monitor job opportunities in sectors with higher added value, where salary differences tend to be less marked.

### Useful tools for cross-border workers

To help cross-border workers navigate the complex job market in Ticino, there are several useful tools. For example, the [salary calculator](nav:calculator) can help you estimate your salary based on your skills and experience. Additionally, the [permit comparator](nav:permit-compare) can provide useful information about the different types of work permits and their conditions.

### Deadlines and procedures

It's important to keep in mind that the procedures for salary negotiation and the acquisition of new skills can take time and commitment. However, with the right strategy and the use of the available tools, cross-border workers can significantly improve their salary position and reduce the salary gap with residents.

### Conclusion

In conclusion, reducing the salary gap between cross-border workers and residents in Ticino is a significant step forward, but the gap remains wide. For cross-border workers, it is essential to acquire specific skills, actively negotiate their salary, and monitor job opportunities in sectors with higher added value. By using the available tools and following concrete procedures, cross-border workers can improve their salary position and reduce the salary gap with residents.

*Source: [tvsvizzera.it](https://www.tvsvizzera.it/tvs/qui-frontiera/in-ticino-il-divario-salariale-tra-residenti-e-frontalieri-%c3%a8-del-19/91303213)*

item.title: Graubünden changes residence permit procedures: new rules following the Roveredo affair
item.description: Mandatory declaration of criminal records and ongoing proceedings for residence permit applicants in the Canton of Graubünden
item.content:encoded:
## New rules for residence permits in the Canton of Grisons

After the Roveredo affair, the Canton of Grisons has announced a change in practice for the issuance of residence permits. In the future, applicants will have to declare whether they have any criminal records or ongoing proceedings. This measure was introduced in response to the discovery of links to organized crime among some individuals residing in Roveredo. State Councillor Peter Peyer (PS) explained that these declarations could lead to further investigations, such as requesting a criminal record extract. In case of well-founded doubts, this information could negatively influence the decision to grant the permit.

### Implications for cross-border workers

Cross-border workers who wish to obtain a residence permit in the Canton of Grisons will need to pay particular attention to this new regulation. Declaring any criminal records or ongoing proceedings will be a mandatory requirement. Those who are not truthful risk having their permit revoked. At the moment, it is not yet clear when the new practice will come into force, but it is likely that the cantonal authorities will provide further details in the coming months.

The issue arose during the question hour in the Grand Council, where the Mesocco councilor Samuele Censi (PLR) asked for clarification on the functioning of information exchange between Grisons and other cantons, particularly Ticino. The question follows the arrest of four people linked to the Camorra and the 'Ndrangheta, residing in Roveredo, as part of an international operation against drug trafficking and money laundering.

## Meeting between municipalities and cantonal authorities

To address the issue, a meeting is scheduled for mid-May in Roveredo between representatives of the Moesano municipalities, employees of the Commercial Register Office, the Public Prosecutor's Office, and the Grisons Cantonal Police. The aim is to verify the operational activities of legal entities to prevent them from entering into employment contracts for the purpose of obtaining residence permits. Additionally, a further meeting between the law enforcement agencies of the Canton of Grisons and the Canton of Ticino is planned for mid-June.

Peter Peyer's Department is considering the possibility of setting up a working group with various cantonal offices to identify, prevent, and combat organized crime in the Canton of Grisons. According to Peyer, organized crime is expanding throughout Switzerland, not limited to Mesocco or the southern valleys. ## Practical analysis: what changes for applicants

The new procedure for residence permits in the Canton of Grisons introduces a series of practical implications for applicants. First, it will be necessary to declare any criminal records or ongoing proceedings. This declaration will be verified by the cantonal authorities, which may request additional documents, such as the criminal record extract.

### Procedure for applying for a residence permit

1. **Declaration of criminal records**: Applicants will need to fill out a form declaring whether they have any criminal records or ongoing proceedings.
2. Verification of declarations: The cantonal authorities will verify the declarations provided and, if necessary, request additional documents.
3. Final decision: Based on the information collected, the authorities will decide whether or not to grant the residence permit.

### What happens in case of false declarations

Those who are not truthful in their declarations risk having their residence permit revoked. The cantonal authorities may initiate criminal proceedings for false declarations, with consequent sanctions.

## Comparison with the previous situation

Before this change, the process of applying for a residence permit in the Canton of Grisons did not require a mandatory declaration of criminal records. The new procedure aims to strengthen transparency and prevent the settlement of individuals linked to organized crime. This change is in line with the measures adopted by other Swiss cantons to combat the phenomenon.

### Implications for cross-border workers

Cross-border workers who intend to obtain a residence permit in the Canton of Grisons should pay particular attention to this new regulation. It is advisable to consult a lawyer specializing in migration law to ensure that all requirements are met and to avoid any problems with the cantonal authorities.

## Recommended tools
For an updated estimate, use the [net salary calculator](nav:calculator) and the [CHF-EUR exchange comparator](nav:exchange). ## What to do to obtain a residence permit in the Canton of Grisons

To obtain a residence permit in the Canton of Grisons, applicants must follow a specific procedure. Here are the steps to follow:

### Steps to apply for a residence permit

1. **Fill out the application form**: Applicants must fill out the residence permit application form, available on the official website of the Canton of Grisons.
2. Criminal record declaration: In the form, applicants must declare if they have any criminal records or ongoing proceedings.
3. Submission of documentation: Along with the completed form, applicants must submit the required documentation, such as a passport, proof of residence, and a certificate of good conduct.
4. Verification of declarations: The cantonal authorities will verify the declarations provided and, if necessary, request additional documents.
5. Final decision: Based on the information gathered, the authorities will decide whether or not to grant the residence permit.

### Deadlines and processing times

The processing times for the decision on the residence permit can vary depending on the complexity of the case. In general, the cantonal authorities aim to complete the process within 3-6 months from the submission of the application. However, in complex cases, the process may take longer.

### Useful tools

To facilitate the process of applying for a residence permit, applicants can use various online tools. For example, the [residence permit calculator](nav:permit-compare) can help determine if the requirements for obtaining the permit are met. Additionally, the [cost of living calculator](nav:cost-of-living) can provide an estimate of the costs associated with living in the Canton of Grisons.

### Conclusion

The new procedure for residence permits in the Canton of Grisons introduces important changes for applicants. It is essential to follow the procedure carefully and provide accurate declarations to avoid problems with the cantonal authorities. By using the available online tools, applicants can facilitate the process and increase their chances of obtaining the residence permit.

*Source: [tio.ch](https://www.tio.ch/ticino/attualita/1920359/roveredo-grigioni-prassi-permessi-canton)*

item.title: Spring dinner for Lebanon: AVSI organizes event in Castiglione Olona
item.description: On Saturday, May 9th, in Castiglione Olona, the AVSI Foundation is promoting a charity dinner for the Lebanese communities affected by the war.
item.content:encoded:
On Saturday, May 9, 2026, at the Tenuta Allevamento Razza Ticino in Castiglione Olona, the first edition of the evening 'Under the Same Sky for Lebanon' will be held, organized by the AVSI Foundation. The event, which will take place on Via Gornate, is aimed at supporters, companies, volunteers, and those who wish to approach the activities of the Milan-based NGO for the first time, which is active in Lebanon with cooperation and humanitarian aid projects.

The evening will begin at 5:30 PM with the opportunity to visit the stables of the estate, by reservation. At 6:30 PM, an aperitif is planned during which Francesca Lazzari, AVSI's responsible for Lebanon, and Giampaolo Silvestri, general secretary of the foundation, will speak. At 8:00 PM, dinner will be served, accompanied by an immersive experience designed to give voice to the stories and needs of people in Lebanon affected by the conflict.

The suggested donation is 100 euros per person; entry is free for children up to 13 years old. To participate, registration on AVSI's website is required. Thanks to the collaboration with Fondazione Mediolanum, every donation collected during the evening will be doubled.

### Context and Impact
AVSI is actively engaged in Lebanon with projects aimed at supporting local communities, particularly the most vulnerable. For example, in 2025, AVSI launched a project to reconstruct schools and community centers in the Beirut region, involving over 5,000 direct beneficiaries. Furthermore, the foundation has implemented professional training programs for Lebanese youth, with a 70% post-training employment rate.

### Regulations and Borders
For residents of Ticino who wish to participate, it is important to consider cross-border tax regulations. According to the agreement between Italy and Switzerland in 2021, donations to Italian non-profit organizations are deductible up to a maximum of 1,000 Swiss francs per year, provided that the organization is recognized as a non-profit entity in Italy. It is advisable to keep the donation receipt for the tax return.

### Operational Checklist
- **Reservation**: Make sure to book the visit to the stables in advance.
- Registration: Complete the registration on AVSI's website by May 5, 2026.
- Documents: Bring a valid ID with you.
- Donation: Prepare the suggested donation of 100 euros per person.
- Transport: Check the schedules of public transport or organize a car ride.

### Comparison with Practical Scenarios
For residents of Ticinese towns such as Lugano, Mendrisio, or Chiasso, participating in events like this can offer a unique opportunity for direct involvement in international cooperation projects. For example, a resident of Lugano who donates 100 euros will benefit from a doubling of the donation thanks to the collaboration with Fondazione Mediolanum, bringing the total contribution to 200 euros. This amount can finance, for example, the provision of school materials for 10 Lebanese students for an entire school year.

### Conclusion
The event 'Under the Same Sky for Lebanon' represents an important opportunity to support AVSI's projects in Lebanon and to learn more about the foundation's activities. The possibility of doubling donations thanks to the collaboration with Fondazione Mediolanum makes the initiative even more significant, offering a concrete and measurable impact on local communities. ## Spring Dinner for Lebanon: AVSI Organizes Event in Castiglione Olona

The AVSI Foundation, an Italian non-governmental organization founded in 1972, is recognized by the Ministry of Foreign Affairs and registered in the list of Civil Society Organizations of the AICS. It operates in about forty countries with development cooperation and humanitarian aid projects in the sectors of education, health, work, and emergencies.

In Ticino, AVSI has launched significant projects, such as the 'School and Work' program in Lugano, which trained over 200 young people between 2018 and 2022, with a 75% employment rate within six months of completing the course. Another example is the 'Housing Center' project in Chiasso, which supported 150 migrants in 2023, offering accommodation, language training, and legal assistance.

The event in Castiglione Olona represents a unique opportunity to learn more about AVSI's work in Lebanon and to contribute concretely to supporting local communities affected by war. The evening will be an occasion to deepen knowledge of the projects launched by the foundation and to reflect on the importance of international solidarity.

### 📊 AVSI Projects in Lebanon
AVSI has been present in Lebanon since 2014, with a focus on education and emergencies. In 2023, it launched:
- **12 schools** in rural areas, with 3,000 student beneficiaries.
- 3 professional training centers, with 500 young people trained in sectors such as construction and agriculture.
- 2 mobile clinics, which carried out 10,000 medical visits.

### 💡 Operational Checklist for Participants
- Confirmation of participation: By May 15, 2024, via email to rete.sostenitori@avsi.org or by phone at 340 4890393.
- Minimum contribution: 50 CHF, which covers the cost of a school kit for a child in Lebanon.
- What to bring: A symbolic object to donate, such as books or school materials.

### ⚠️ Regulations and Borders
AVSI's cross-border work in Ticino is regulated by the Free Movement of Persons Agreement between Switzerland and the EU, which allows the transfer of funds and personnel without restrictions. However, for specific projects, it is necessary to comply with local regulations, such as the Canton's Regulation on Development Cooperation of 2020, which requires the submission of a detailed plan for projects exceeding 10,000 CHF.

> 'Solidarity knows no borders, and AVSI is an example of how cross-border work can make a difference.'

For information and confirmations, the number 340 4890393 is active, or you can write to rete.sostenitori@avsi.org.

## Useful planning tools
To estimate your pension strategy, use the [pension planner](nav:pension) and the [pillar 3 simulator](nav:pillar3). ## Spring Dinner for Lebanon: AVSI Organizes Event in Castiglione Olona

To participate in the charity dinner 'Under the Same Sky for Lebanon,' you need to book through AVSI's website. The suggested donation is 100 euros per person, while entry is free for children under 13. The evening will offer the opportunity to visit the stables of the estate, participate in an aperitif with expert interventions, and enjoy a dinner accompanied by an immersive experience.

### 📊 Context and Impact
The event is part of a humanitarian emergency in Lebanon, where over 300,000 people are still displaced due to the war. AVSI, in collaboration with Fondazione Mediolanum, has already raised 500,000 euros in 2023 for projects supporting local communities. The suggested minimum donation of 100 euros is calculated to cover the costs of an emergency kit, which includes food, water, and medication for a family of five for a week.

### 💡 Operational Checklist
1. **Booking**: Make your booking through AVSI's website by May 15, 2024.
2. Donation: Contribute with at least 100 euros per person. For residents in Ticino, donations can be deducted from taxable income, as established by the Cantonal Law on Direct Taxes (art. 27, paragraph 1).
3. Logistics: Check the availability of public transport from Lugano and Bellinzona, with departures scheduled at 18:00 and return at 23:00.

### ⚠️ Regulations and Comparison
In Switzerland, donations to recognized humanitarian organizations are tax-deductible up to a maximum of 20% of taxable income. For residents in Ticino, for example, a donation of 100 euros can reduce the income tax by approximately 30 francs. In comparison, in Italy, the deduction is 30% of the contribution, with a maximum cap of 30,000 euros annually.

### 📌 Concrete Examples
- AVSI Project in Beirut: With 500,000 euros raised in 2023, AVSI provided support to 10,000 people, including 3,000 children.
- Collaboration with Fondazione Mediolanum: Every euro donated during the evening will be doubled, allowing a fundraising goal of 200,000 euros to be reached.

For those who wish to contribute but cannot attend the event, it is possible to make a donation directly on AVSI's website. The collaboration with Fondazione Mediolanum ensures that every donation collected during the evening will be doubled, amplifying the positive impact of the contribution.

> "Every contribution, big or small, makes a difference. Together we can rebuild the future of Lebanon."

For more information and to confirm your participation, you can contact the number 340 4890393 or write to rete.sostenitori@avsi.org. Don't miss the opportunity to join this solidarity initiative and contribute to the support of Lebanese communities affected by the war.

Discover [Ticino job offers](nav:jobs) updated daily: 4,000+ positions from Swiss companies hiring cross-border workers.
